stimulus:
Press release: A comprehensive review evaluating the
medical studies done up to the present time has
found no reason to think that drinking coffee
in normal amounts harms the coffee-drinker’s
heart. So coffee drinkers can relax and enjoy their
beverage—it is safe to drink coffee.
questionStem: Which one of the following points to a weakness in the reasoning in the press release’s argument?

answerChoices.A: The review was only an evaluation of studies and did not itself undertake to study patients.
answerChoices.B: The health of the heart is not identical with the general health of the body.
answerChoices.C: Coffee drinkers might choose to eat, along with their coffee, foods containing substances that harm the heart.
answerChoices.D: Other beverages besides coffee might contain stimulants that have some effect on the heart.
answerChoices.E: Drinking unusually large amounts of coffee could be caused by stress that itself directly harms the heart.

stimulus:
All people prefer colors that they can distinguish easily to
colors that they have difficulty distinguishing. Infants can
easily distinguish bright colors but, unlike adults, have
difficulty distinguishing subtle shades. A brightly colored
toy for infants sells better than the same toy in subtle
shades at the same price.
questionStem:
Which one of the following conclusions is most strongly
supported by the information in the passage?

answerChoices.A: Infants prefer bright primary colors to bright secondary colors.
answerChoices.B: Color is the most important factor in determining which toys an infant will prefer to play with.
answerChoices.C: Individual infants do not have strong preferences for one particular bright color over other bright colors.
answerChoices.D: The sales of toys for infants reflect the preferences of infants in at least one respect.
answerChoices.E: Toy makers study infants to determine what colors the infants can distinguish easily.

stimulus:
A group of unusual meteorites was found in Shergotty,
India. Their structure indicates that they originated on
one of the geologically active planets, Mercury, Venus,
or Mars. Because of Mercury’s proximity to the Sun,
any material dislodged from that planet’s surface would
have been captured by the Sun, rather than falling to
Earth as meteorites. Nor could Venus be the source of
the meteorites, because its gravity would have prevented
dislodged material from escaping into space. The
meteorites, therefore, probably fell to Earth after being
dislodged from Mars, perhaps as the result of a collision
with a large object.
questionStem: The argument derives its conclusion by

answerChoices.A: offering a counterexample to a theory
answerChoices.B: eliminating competing alternative explanations
answerChoices.C: contrasting present circumstances with past circumstances
answerChoices.D: questioning an assumption
answerChoices.E: abstracting a general principle from specific data

stimulus:
Because quitting smoking is very stressful and leads
to weight gain, it is difficult to do. The key to quitting,
however, may be as simple as replacing an unhealthy
activity with a healthy one. In one study, half of those
attempting to quit were assigned to a smoking-cessation
program alone, and the other half were assigned to the
same program plus fifteen weeks of aerobic exercise. At
the one-month mark, none in the first group had quit, but
40 percent of those in the second group had not smoked.
questionStem:
Each of the following, if true, provides some support for
the argument except :

answerChoices.A: Regular exercise prevents weight gain.
answerChoices.B: Each group in the study included four hundred randomly selected participants.
answerChoices.C: Nonsmokers accustomed to regular exercise do not gain weight when they stop exercising.
answerChoices.D: Aerobic exercise can stimulate the brain’s production of endorphins, which reduce tension.
answerChoices.E: Of those in the second group in the study, 38 percent had not smoked at the one-year mark.

stimulus:
Altogether, the students in Ms. Tarnowski’s Milton
Elementary School class collected more aluminum cans
than did the students in any of the school’s other classes.
Therefore, the Milton student who collected the most
aluminum cans was in Ms. Tarnowski’s class.
questionStem:
Which one of the following arguments contains flawed
reasoning that is most parallel to that in the argument
above?

answerChoices.A: Altogether, more trees were planted by the students in Mr. Kelly’s class than were planted by those in Mr. Liang’s class and Ms. Jackson’s class combined. Therefore, Mr. Kelly’s students planted more trees than Ms. Jackson’s students planted.
answerChoices.B: More than half of Milton Elementary School’s students play in the band and more than half of the school’s students sing in the choir. Therefore, every student at Milton Elementary School either plays in the band or sings in the choir.
answerChoices.C: Mr. Rowe’s Milton Elementary School class raised more money by selling candy bars than Ms. Hunt’s class raised by holding a raffle. Therefore, the number of candy bars sold by Mr. Rowe’s class was greater than the number of raffle tickets sold by Ms. Hunt’s class.
answerChoices.D: The total number of tickets to the school fair sold by the students in Ms. Ramirez’s Milton Elementary School class was greater than the number sold by Milton students from any other class. Therefore, the Milton student who sold the most tickets to the school fair was a student in Ms. Ramirez’s class.
answerChoices.E: Ms. Ventura’s Milton Elementary School class assembled more birdhouses than did any of the school’s other classes. Since Ms. Ventura’s class had fewer students than any other Milton class, her students assembled more birdhouses, on average, than did the students in any other Milton class.

stimulus:
Several excellent candidates have been proposed for
the presidency of United Wire, and each candidate
would bring to the job different talents and experience.
If the others are compared with Jones, however, it will
be apparent that none of them has her unique set of
qualifications. Jones, therefore, is best qualified to be the
new president of United Wire.
questionStem:
The argument is vulnerable to criticism on the ground
that it

answerChoices.A: uses flattery to win over those who hold an opposing position
answerChoices.B: refutes a distorted version of an opposing position
answerChoices.C: seeks to distinguish one member of a group on the basis of something that applies to all
answerChoices.D: supports a universal claim on the basis of a single example
answerChoices.E: describes an individual in terms that appropriately refer only to the group as a whole

stimulus: Residents of the city of Krisville have been protesting the very poor state of the city’s recreation facilities. In response, city officials plan to build ten new recreation centers, one in each of the city’s ten neighborhoods. These centers will include a range of exercise facilities. City officials argue that when these facilities are available, many residents will begin to get the regular exercise they need, thereby improving their health; this increase in the health of the population will result in a decrease in the city’s per capita medical costs. So although the centers will be expensive to build, they will ultimately cause the city’s per capita medical costs to decrease.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

answerChoices.A: Because recreation centers are distributed among many neighborhoods, it is not time-consuming for residents to get to and from a center.
answerChoices.B: The exercise machines in the new recreation centers will be convenient and easy to use.
answerChoices.C: Krisville’s recreation centers will be scheduled so as to minimize conflicts between different age groups.
answerChoices.D: Most residents who have been exercising regularly for at least a year belong to private health clubs.
answerChoices.E: In other cities where recreation centers have been provided, the per capita medical costs have decreased.

stimulus: of violent crimes in our society is false, for this claim is based upon the large number of stories in newspapers about violent crimes. But since violent crimes are very rare occurrences, newspapers are likely to print stories about them.
questionStem: The sociologist’s argument is flawed because it

answerChoices.A: presupposes that most newspaper stories are about violent crime
answerChoices.B: presupposes the truth of the conclusion it is attempting to establish
answerChoices.C: assumes without warrant that the newspaper stories in question are not biased
answerChoices.D: mistakes a property of each member of a group taken as an individual for a property of the group taken as a whole
answerChoices.E: uncritically draws an inference from what has been true in the past to what will be true in the future

stimulus:
Historian: Anyone who thinks that the terrors of the ancient regime of Q were exclusively the work of fanatics is overlooking a basic truth: the regime was made up primarily of ordinary people enthusiastically seeking paradise. The regime executed many people in pursuit of its goal; but it later became clear that paradise,
as they defined it, is unrealizable. So at least some of the ordinary people of Q were in fact murderers.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, provides the most support for the historian’s argumentation?

answerChoices.A: The pursuit of paradise does not justify murder.
answerChoices.B: The pursuit of paradise justifies fanaticism.
answerChoices.C: Execution in pursuit of what is later found to be unattainable constitutes murder.
answerChoices.D: Fanaticism in pursuit of paradise constitutes inhumanity.
answerChoices.E: Enthusiasm in pursuit of what is eventually found to be unattainable constitutes fanaticism.

stimulus:
Economist: The economy seems to be heading out of recession. Recent figures show that consumers
are buying more durable goods than before, indicating that they expect economic growth in the near future.
questionStem: That consumers are buying more durable goods than before figures in the economist’s argument in which one of the following ways?

answerChoices.A: It is the phenomenon that the argument seeks to explain.
answerChoices.B: Its truth is required in order for the argument’s conclusion to be true.
answerChoices.C: It is an inference drawn from the premise that the recession seems to be ending.
answerChoices.D: It is an inference drawn from the premise that consumers expect economic growth in the near future.
answerChoices.E: It is the primary evidence from which the argument’s conclusion is drawn.

stimulus: Not surprisingly, there are no professors under the age of eighteen. And, as is well known, no one under eighteen can vote legally. Finally, some brilliant people are professors, some are legal voters, and some are under eighteen.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, then on the basis of them which one of the following must also be true?

answerChoices.A: No professors are eighteen-year-olds.
answerChoices.B: All brilliant people are either professors, legal voters, or under eighteen.
answerChoices.C: Some legal voters are not professors.
answerChoices.D: Some professors are neither legal voters nor brilliant people.
answerChoices.E: Some brilliant people are neither professors nor legal voters.

stimulus: For years scientists have been scanning the skies in search of life elsewhere in the universe. To date there has been no success in this endeavor. The budget for the project of searching for extraterrestrial life is being reduced, and critics argue that the budget should be reduced even further.
questionStem: Which one of the following findings would be most relevant to deciding whether the budget of the project should be reduced?

answerChoices.A: The number of newly discovered planets has been increasing over the last few years.
answerChoices.B: The project sometimes shares its funds with other space-research programs.
answerChoices.C: The number of new planets that have been discovered has been dropping in recent years.
answerChoices.D: The number of scientists working on the project has been increasing steadily.
answerChoices.E: The cost of the project has been increasing faster than the overall cost of space research.

stimulus: There are whitecaps on the ocean of Venus. Hence there must be wind on Venus, since there cannot be whitecaps without wind.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

answerChoices.A: The surface of Venus is mostly hot, dry land.
answerChoices.B: Curves of shadows across Venus from the telescope’s rectangular aperture produce an optical illusion that can make Venus’s land surface appear ruffled like a stormy sea.
answerChoices.C: The planet Venus has no atmosphere.
answerChoices.D: Only if there exist wavelike disturbances in the clouds of Venus is it possible that the winds on Venus blow in one direction and thus could be responsible for some or all of the observed evidence that someone might describe as “whitecaps.”
answerChoices.E: The cloud cover on Venus is often broken, allowing occasional glimpses of the planet’s surface.

stimulus: In an effort to boost sales during the summer months, which are typically the best for soft-drink sales, Foamy Soda lowered its prices. In spite of this, however, the sales of Foamy Soda dropped during the summer months.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, contributes to reconciling the apparent discrepancy indicated above except:

answerChoices.A: The soft-drink industry as a whole experienced depressed sales during the summer months.
answerChoices.B: Foamy Soda’s competitors lowered their prices even more drastically during the summer months.
answerChoices.C: Because of an increase in the price of sweeteners, the production costs of Foamy Soda rose during the summer months.
answerChoices.D: A strike at Foamy Soda’s main plant forced production cutbacks that resulted in many stores not receiving their normal shipments during the summer months.
answerChoices.E: The weather during the summer months was unseasonably cool, decreasing the demand for soft drinks.

stimulus: Dr. Z: Many of the characterizations of my work offered by Dr. Q are imprecise, and such characterizations do not provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of my work.
questionStem: Which one of the following can be properly inferred from Dr. Z’s statement?

answerChoices.A: Some of Dr. Q’s characterizations of Dr. Z’s work provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of Dr. Z’s work.
answerChoices.B: All of Dr. Q’s characterizations of Dr. Z’s work that are not imprecise provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of Dr. Z’s work.
answerChoices.C: All of the characterizations of Dr. Z’s work by Dr. Q that do not provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of Dr. Z’s work are imprecise.
answerChoices.D: If the characterization of someone’s work is precise, then it provides a sound basis for criticizing that work.
answerChoices.E: At least one of Dr. Q’s characterizations of Dr. Z’s work fails to provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of that work.

stimulus: K, a research scientist, was accused of having falsified laboratory data. Although the original data in question have disappeared, data from K’s more recent experiments have been examined and clearly none of them were falsified. Therefore, the accusation should be dismissed.
questionStem: Which one of the following contains questionable reasoning that is most similar to that in the argument above?

answerChoices.A: L, an accountant, was charged with having embezzled funds from a client. The charge should be ignored, however, because although the records that might reveal this embezzlement have been destroyed, records of L’s current clients show clearly that there has never been any embezzlement from them.
answerChoices.B: M, a factory supervisor, was accused of failing to enforce safety standards. This accusation should be discussed, because although the identity of the accuser was not revealed, a survey of factory personnel revealed that some violations of the standards have occurred.
answerChoices.C: N, a social scientist, was charged with plagiarism. The charge is without foundation because although strong similarities between N’s book and the work of another scholar have been discovered, the other scholar’s work was written after N’s work was published.
answerChoices.D: O, an auto mechanic, has been accused of selling stolen auto parts. The accusation seems to be justified since although no evidence links O directly to these sales, the pattern of distribution of the auto parts points to O as the source.
answerChoices.E: P, a politician, has been accused of failing to protect the public interest. From at least some points of view, however, the accusation will undoubtedly be considered false, because there is clearly disagreement about where the public interest lies.

stimulus: An economic downturn has recently affected the region of Krisville and nearby areas, and therefore many citizens have lost their jobs. Storeowners in Krisville expected that people would buy fewer goods and that sales in Krisville stores would decline. However, it is now reported that the use of credit cards in Krisville has dramatically increased. This would not occur if people were purchasing fewer goods, since the only way of paying for goods with a credit card is to purchase those goods. Therefore, it is likely that sales in Krisville stores have not declined.
questionStem: The argument in the passage proceeds by doing which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: concluding that since an expected consequence of a supposed development did not occur, that development itself did not take place
answerChoices.B: concluding that since only one of the two predictable consequences of a certain kind of behavior is observed to occur, this observed occurrence cannot, in the current situation, be a consequence of such behavior
answerChoices.C: arguing that since people’s economic behavior is guided by economic self-interest, only misinformation or error will cause people to engage in economic behavior that harms them economically
answerChoices.D: arguing that since two alternative developments exhaust all the plausible possibilities, one of those developments occurred and the other did not
answerChoices.E: concluding that since the evidence concerning a supposed change is ambiguous, it is most likely that no change is actually taking place

stimulus:
An economic downturn has recently affected the region of Krisville and nearby areas, and therefore many citizens have lost their jobs. Storeowners in Krisville expected that people would buy fewer goods and that sales in Krisville stores would decline. However, it is now reported that the use of credit cards in Krisville
has dramatically increased. This would not occur if people were purchasing fewer goods, since the only way of paying for goods with a credit card is to purchase those goods. Therefore, it is likely that sales in Krisville stores have not declined.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies?

answerChoices.A: If people in the region who continue to be employed have debts, they are not now paying them off at an accelerated rate.
answerChoices.B: People in the region who continue to be employed and who have relatives who have lost their jobs commonly assist those relatives financially.
answerChoices.C: If people in the region who have lost jobs get new jobs, the new jobs generally pay less well than the ones they lost.
answerChoices.D: People in the region who continue to be employed are pessimistic about their prospects for increasing their incomes.
answerChoices.E: There exist no statistics about sales of goods in the region as a whole.

stimulus: Every student who walks to school goes home for lunch. It follows that some students who have part-time jobs do not walk to school.
questionStem: The conclusion of the argument follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

answerChoices.A: Some students who do not have part-time jobs go home for lunch.
answerChoices.B: Every student who goes home for lunch has a part-time job.
answerChoices.C: Some students who do not have part-time jobs do not go home for lunch.
answerChoices.D: Some students who do not go home for lunch have part-time jobs.
answerChoices.E: Every student who goes home for lunch walks to school.

stimulus: When the Pinecrest Animal Shelter, a charitable organization, was in danger of closing because it could not pay for important repairs, its directors appealed to the townspeople to donate money that would be earmarked to pay for those repairs. Since more funds were ultimately donated than were used for the repairs, the directors plan to donate the surplus funds to other animal shelters. But before doing so, the directors should obtain permission from those who made the donations.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the position advocated above and yet places the least restriction on the allocation of funds by directors of charitable organizations?

answerChoices.A: The directors of charitable organizations cannot allocate publicly solicited funds to any purposes for which the directors had not specifically earmarked the funds in advance.
answerChoices.B: People who solicit charitable donations from the public for a specific cause should spend the funds only on that cause or, if that becomes impossible, should dispose of the funds according to the express wishes of the donors.
answerChoices.C: A charitable organization that has solicited funds for a specific cause must return to the donors any funds not spent on that cause unless the donors permit the organization to spend the funds for other purposes.
answerChoices.D: Charitable organizations should not solicit funds for specific causes unless they can guarantee that the funds raised will be spent only on those causes.
answerChoices.E: Charitable organizations should be allowed to allocate funds as they see fit, provided the funds are spent for charitable purposes.

stimulus: Newspaper editor: Law enforcement experts, as well as most citizens, have finally come to recognize that legal prohibitions against gambling all share a common flaw: no matter how diligent the effort, the laws are impossible to enforce. Ethical qualms notwithstanding, when a law fails to be effective, it should not be a law. That is why there should be no legal prohibition against gambling.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if assumed, allows the argument’s conclusion to be properly drawn?

answerChoices.A: No effective law is unenforceable.
answerChoices.B: All enforceable laws are effective.
answerChoices.C: No legal prohibitions against gambling are enforceable.
answerChoices.D: Most citizens must agree with a law for the law to be effective.
answerChoices.E: Most citizens must agree with a law for the law to be enforceable.

stimulus:
Copernicus’s astronomical system is superior to Ptolemy’s and was so at the time it was proposed, even though at that time all observational evidence was equally consistent with both theories. Ptolemy believed that the stars revolved around the earth at great speeds.
This struck Copernicus as unlikely; he correctly thought that a simpler theory is that the earth rotates on its axis.
questionStem: The argument most closely conforms to which one of the following principles?

answerChoices.A: Simplicity should be the sole deciding factor in choosing among competing scientific theories.
answerChoices.B: If one theory is likely to be true, and another competing theory is likely to be false, then the one likely to be true is the superior of the two.
answerChoices.C: If all observational evidence is consistent with two competing theories, the one that is more intuitively true is the more practical theory to adopt.
answerChoices.D: Other things being equal, the more complex of two competing theories is the inferior theory.
answerChoices.E: Other things being equal, the simpler of two competing theories is the more scientifically important theory.

stimulus: Essayist: The existence of a moral order in the universe—i.e., an order in which bad is always eventually punished and good rewarded—depends upon human souls being immortal. In some cultures this moral order is regarded as the result of a karma that controls how one is reincarnated, in others it results from the actions of a supreme being who metes out justice to people after their death. But however a moral order is represented, if human souls are immortal, then it follows that the bad will be punished.
questionStem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the essayist’s reasoning?

answerChoices.A: From the assertion that something is necessary to a moral order, the argument concludes that that thing is sufficient for an element of the moral order to be realized.
answerChoices.B: The argument takes mere beliefs to be established facts.
answerChoices.C: From the claim that the immortality of human souls implies that there is a moral order in the universe, the argument concludes that there being a moral order in the universe implies that human souls are immortal.
answerChoices.D: The argument treats two fundamentally different conceptions of a moral order as essentially the same.
answerChoices.E: The argument’s conclusion is presupposed in the definition it gives of a moral order.

stimulus: No mathematical proposition can be proven true by observation. It follows that it is impossible to know any mathematical proposition to be true.
questionStem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

answerChoices.A: Only propositions that can be proven true can be known to be true.
answerChoices.B: Observation alone cannot be used to prove the truth of any proposition.
answerChoices.C: If a proposition can be proven true by observation, then it can be known to be true.
answerChoices.D: Knowing a proposition to be true is impossible only if it cannot be proven true by observation.
answerChoices.E: Knowing a proposition to be true requires proving it true by observation.

stimulus: The publisher of a best-selling self-help book had, in some promotional material, claimed that it showed readers how to become exceptionally successful. Of course, everyone knows that no book can deliver to the many what, by definition, must remain limited to the few: exceptional success. Thus, although it is clear that the publisher knowingly made a false claim, doing so should not be considered unethical in this case.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most strongly supports the reasoning above?

answerChoices.A: Knowingly making a false claim is unethical only if it is reasonable for people to accept the claim as true.
answerChoices.B: Knowingly making a false claim is unethical if those making it derive a gain at the expense of those acting as if the claim were true.
answerChoices.C: Knowingly making a false claim is unethical only if those making it expect that most people will accept the claim as true.
answerChoices.D: Knowingly making a false claim is ethical only if those making it have good intentions.
answerChoices.E: Knowingly making a false claim is always unethical.
